response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental incompatibility between your .NET 7 cross-platform main application and the WPF library, which is designed exclusively for .NET Framework on Windows. When your code attempts to load the WPF assembly, the .NET 7 runtime looks for a version compatible with its own architecture but fails because the specific version string `4.0.0.0` refers to the legacy .NET Framework implementation that is not present in the .NET 7 environment.

To resolve this without reverting your main application to .NET Framework, you must ensure that the WPF project is explicitly configured to target .NET 7 or later, rather than the older .NET Framework. This involves updating the `TargetFramework` property in your WPF project file to `net7.0-windows`, which allows the WPF components to be compiled and loaded as part of the modern .NET 7 ecosystem while still providing the necessary UI functionality on Windows.

cousin projects in your solution structure often cause confusion when they target different runtimes, so it is crucial to verify that your build configuration correctly isolates the WPF dependency for Windows builds only. You should utilize conditional compilation directives or project references that are only active when the `$(TargetFramework)` matches a Windows-specific identifier, ensuring that the WPF assembly is never referenced or expected during the macOS build process where Cocoa handles the UI.

Another effective strategy is to use a dynamic loading approach where the WPF window is instantiated only after checking the operating system at runtime using `System.Runtime.InteropServices.RuntimeInformation`. By wrapping the WPF initialization code inside a guard clause that verifies the OS is Windows, you prevent the .NET 7 runtime from attempting to load the WPF assembly on macOS, thereby avoiding the file not found exception entirely while maintaining your cross-platform architecture.

Finally, ensure that your project references are clean and that you have removed any direct references to the legacy `PresentationFramework` assembly that might have been added by default when creating the WPF project under an older template. By aligning all projects to target .NET 7 with appropriate platform qualifiers and using conditional logic to manage UI rendering, you can successfully maintain a single codebase that utilizes WPF on Windows and Cocoa on macOS without triggering runtime loading errors.
